text: I slid the coffee table further out of the way and lay down so I could lift the flap and reach beneath the sofa. God knows what I’d find among the dust bunnies, right? Maybe two other glasses, an extra remote I thought I’d lost, nickels or dimes worth even less than when I first dropped them. The glass rolled along the floor like the rumble of wheels over concrete, the scrape of a door sliding open and shut, the regular tap of footsteps. You remember how a glass might be a musical instrument—wet the rim and run your finger around it, and produce a pure tone almost like a bell or a bird singing? That seemed to be happening now, the glass rim rolling on the floor just out of my reach. The sound had a rasp to it, like a harsh whisper. I pulled my phone from my front pocket and aimed the flashlight app under the couch. Lying flat, I strained and stretched until my fingertips brushed against the glass. Eventually I pinched the rim, stopped the motion. But the whisper continued. I heard panic in it. Somebody who was afraid of getting caught. The person who broke into my apartment while I was out, of course. And if that person whispered out loud, that meant there was an accomplice. The two of them had moved my furniture around, searching for something, and I’d surprised them by coming back early. I froze, not sure how to react. If I stayed there lying on the floor, they might not see me. But my position was vulnerable, too, prone on my stomach and easy for anybody to subdue or attack. At the same time, I struggled to distinguish words whispered in the next room, muffled behind my closed bedroom door. The words would have been my best clues, but the whole time I also stared at the cloth flap that hung down from the frame of the sofa, the curtain I wished I could lift and roll beneath to hide forever. Something about the fabric bothered me, the weave of it, the color of the thread. I heard intruder, the address of the building, urgent pleas to send someone quickly, and I wondered why burglars would call the police on themselves. Yet the sofa fabric is what got my brain working. I’ve spent a lot of time on my couch lately. This wasn’t my couch. Finally, right Sis? I know you figured out the punchline a while ago. But I’m trying to explain the fog in my head—that I could hear these people whisper, the rightful owners of the apartment, and think: “They gave the wrong unit number to the police,” rather than that I was in the wrong place. That I walked into a strange living room and tried to put their furniture right, knocked stuff over, crawled on their floor. And the weave of the sofa fabric began to knock sense into me, nothing else. Not enough sense, though. I stood up, looked around the apartment with the same floor plan as my own but every detail different—a two-tone paint scheme, a larger television bolted to the wall, a hexagon shelf with potted plants in each compartment, and that puzzling sofa substitution—and part of me still wondered how burglars could have worked so quickly, and why they would have replaced my furniture rather than simply stealing it. Phantom redecorators, rather than burglars. Go ahead and laugh, Mira, because I was laughing then, too. My laugh must have sounded crazy. After hearing it, I bet the tenants felt more anxious for the police to arrive. That’s when my virus instincts kicked in. I wasn’t alone. I pulled my mask up to cover my mouth and nose, then rubbed another quick squirt of sanitizer over my hands. “I can hear you whispering,” I said to the closed bedroom door. “You can come out now.” I felt brave. I felt like a conflict mediator offering firm, calming advice. To the tenants, my words probably came across as a command. Like I’d said, “Come out here so I can shoot you.” The door wasn’t fully closed. They must have been peering at me through the gap, periodically asking each other, “What’s that guy doing …?” “I see you there,” I said. “I’m wearing a mask. You can come out.” And they did. Slowly. The guy appeared in the gap first, acted like he needed to squeeze his way through. He wasn’t wearing a mask, which angered me a little. I waved my hand, encouraging him to move a little faster. My hand, not a gun. “Other one, too.” I waved my hand again, indicating the woman, and she followed into the living room, staying close beside him. He leaned at an odd angle, like he was trying to cover her whole body with just his shoulder. They looked familiar to me. They should. As I waited for them to emerge from the back bedroom, I’d half-studied a framed picture on the wall. Clueless or vain? my fogged brain wondered. Because: what kind of burglars would leave their own wedding photograph at the crime scene? I was trying to decide what to say to them, and in retrospect they must have pondered the same. They cast furtive glances at each other, around the apartment, at the floor. At me. An uncomfortable silence lasted a minute or ten—hard to tell which—until we were saved by the bell. More accurately, a knock. Three authoritative raps, the kind they teach in the Academy or in cop films. They announced themselves, too, which is standard procedure. Although my presumptuous intruders moved to open the front door, I cut them off and went there myself. “Hello, officers.” I stepped aside to let them enter. It bothered me that the younger officer was bald, and the older one had a full head of dark wavy hair with just a streak of gray. Seemed out of order—and there’s enough
